entries.title: A Word on Olive Oil
entries.summary: Regular olive oil, denoted only as “olive oil” on the label, is oil left from the cold pressing that was too high in acidity to be classified as virgin, so it under-goes a refining process that reduces its acidity. Virgin olive oil is the first oil to be extracted from grinding and pressing olives, a procedure some-times called “cold pressing.” This oil undergoes no further processing. Pomace olive oil (or olive pomace oil) is oil extract-ed from the solid residue, called pomace, that remains after pressing the olives.
takeaways: Regular olive oil, denoted only as “olive oil” on the label, is oil left from the cold pressing that was too high in acidity to be classified as virgin, so it under-goes a refining process that reduces its acidity.
Virgin olive oil is the first oil to be extracted from grinding and pressing olives, a procedure some-times called “cold pressing.” This oil undergoes no further processing.
Pomace olive oil (or olive pomace oil) is oil extract-ed from the solid residue, called pomace, that remains after pressing the olives.
When it’s desirable to use olive oil because of its high mono-unsaturated fat level, but the strong olive flavor is unwanted, pomace or light olive oil is the type to use.
It also has a higher burning point than virgin oil and, so, is used mainly as an all-purpose cooking oil for sautéing.

entries.title: Amylase Content, Wheat Sprouting, and Gummy Layer
entries.summary: The harmful effect of sprouting is an increase of certain enzymes within the wheat, most notably the enzyme alpha-amylase — which increases as much as 5000 times over the amount found in dormant wheat. (An older term for amylase enzymes is “diastase.”) To understand the problem of too much alpha-amylase, we must understand how flour performs with normal amounts of enzyme. However when there’s too much alpha-amylase, as a result of sprouting — or even possibly from add-ing too much diastatic malt product — an enzyme imbalance occurs. In dough, two amylase enzymes — alpha-amylase and beta-amylase — attack the exposed molecules.
takeaways: The harmful effect of sprouting is an increase of certain enzymes within the wheat, most notably the enzyme alpha-amylase — which increases as much as 5000 times over the amount found in dormant wheat. (An older term for amylase enzymes is “diastase.”) To…
However when there’s too much alpha-amylase, as a result of sprouting — or even possibly from add-ing too much diastatic malt product — an enzyme imbalance occurs.
In dough, two amylase enzymes — alpha-amylase and beta-amylase — attack the exposed molecules.
Without the combined action of alpha- and beta-amylase there would be very little maltose for the yeast to digest and, as a result, the pizza crust would be flat and poorly risen.
If, however, a pizzeria receives flour made from an overly high amount of sprouted wheat, the dough will form a gummy crumb or even collapse during baking.

entries.title: Chemical Leaveners
entries.summary: Because salt strengthens gluten, dough with 2 percent salt may require more mixing time than dough with little or no salt, to achieve the same level of gluten development. Other types of sugars that find use in baking are brown sugar (under-refined sucrose), dextrose (corn sugar), honey, molasses, malt, sorghum, and maple sugar. This occurs because sugar delays the coagulation of gluten and gelatini-zation of starch during baking — in effect, causing the crumb to be “less baked” which, in turn, makes it more tender.
takeaways: Because salt strengthens gluten, dough with 2 percent salt may require more mixing time than dough with little or no salt, to achieve the same level of gluten development.
Other types of sugars that find use in baking are brown sugar (under-refined sucrose), dextrose (corn sugar), honey, molasses, malt, sorghum, and maple sugar.
This occurs because sugar delays the coagulation of gluten and gelatini-zation of starch during baking — in effect, causing the crumb to be “less baked” which, in turn, makes it more tender.
During prolonged fermentation other sugars can be nearly consumed by the yeast, which reduces their effect on crust browning, flavor, and tenderness.
So some bakers have replaced salt with a 50:50 mixture of sodium chloride (salt) and potassium chloride.

entries.title: Cream Yeast
entries.summary: REGULAR Active Dry Yeast Regular active dry yeast — commonly called Active Dry Yeast, or ADY for short — has undergone a drying process that reduced its moisture content to about 8 percent. Active dry yeast undergoes slower fermentation than compressed yeast and instant yeast. When rehydrated in colder water it leaches materials (i.e., glutathione) from yeast cells, which results in less fermentation and also produces a stickier, slacker (more extensible) dough.
takeaways: REGULAR Active Dry Yeast Regular active dry yeast — commonly called Active Dry Yeast, or ADY for short — has undergone a drying process that reduced its moisture content to about 8 percent.
Active dry yeast undergoes slower fermentation than compressed yeast and instant yeast.
When rehydrated in colder water it leaches materials (i.e., glutathione) from yeast cells, which results in less fermentation and also produces a stickier, slacker (more extensible) dough.
Rehydration and Usage For rehydration, sprinkle the yeast over 100 to 110 degrees F water, stir thoroughly to dissolve, and let sit for 10 minutes.
Also, increase the dough water by an amount equal to the weight of the dry yeast.

entries.title: Emergency Dough
entries.summary: To save time, some bakeries use no-time dough for regular production, in which case the dough formula usually includes dough conditioners and extra diastatic malt. In this case, double the yeast portion and adjust water tempera-ture so that the dough comes from the mixer at 90 degrees F. Some dough-makers add over-mixed dough and over-fermented dough to new dough during the last minute of mixing.

entries.title: Enzymes (Malt)
entries.summary: Sugar is produced in dough when amylase enzymes attack starch molecules and break them down to sugar — sometimes called “diastatic action.” So, the more amylase there is in the dough, the more sugar is produced and the more fermentation that occurs. There are two groups of malt products: diastatic (enzyme-active) and non-diastatic (enzyme-inactive). Some hard roll and hearth bread formulas call for as much as 5 percent low diastatic malt (based on flour weight) and some cookies contain as much as 33 percent non-diastatic malt.
takeaways: Sugar is produced in dough when amylase enzymes attack starch molecules and break them down to sugar — sometimes called “diastatic action.” So, the more amylase there is in the dough, the more sugar is produced and the more fermentation that occurs.
There are two groups of malt products: diastatic (enzyme-active) and non-diastatic (enzyme-inactive).
Some hard roll and hearth bread formulas call for as much as 5 percent low diastatic malt (based on flour weight) and some cookies contain as much as 33 percent non-diastatic malt.
The original amylase additive is malted barley flour, which is produced by submersing barley grain in warm water until it germinates and then drying it out and grinding it into flour.
In addition to fermentation, other benefits derived from malt syrup and powder are improved crust color, moister crumb, and a sweet, mild malt flavor.

entries.title: Fermentation
entries.summary: These enzymes act to condition the gluten, or make it more extensible, as long as the dough’s salt content is under 1 percent. (Over 1 percent salt inhibits the protein-decomposing action by enzymes.) If simple sugars aren’t available, yeast can decom-pose more complex sugars — namely, sucrose and maltose — into glucose for fermentation. It can be included in the dough recipe and/or it can be created within the dough when amylase enzymes in the dough decompose the starch into maltose. entries.title: Flour Protein
entries.summary: When flour is mixed with water the protein forms gluten — a substance which gives firmness to baked goods. Generally speaking, protein content is related to the hardness of the wheat kernel, such that hard wheats produce flour with moderate-to-high protein content and soft wheats produce flour with low protein content. Generally speaking, the more protein in the flour, the more elastic the dough will be and the firmer the final baked product will be.
takeaways: When flour is mixed with water the protein forms gluten — a substance which gives firmness to baked goods.

Generally speaking, protein content is related to the hardness of the wheat kernel, such that hard wheats produce flour with moderate-to-high protein content and soft wheats produce flour with low protein content.
Generally speaking, the more protein in the flour, the more elastic the dough will be and the firmer the final baked product will be.
Most yeast breads, including pizza crust, are best when made from higher protein flour (at least 11 percent or more protein).
A flour with a higher protein content but of low quality may not perform as well as a flour with slightly less protein but of higher quality.

entries.title: Gluten Relaxers – Protease
entries.summary: Enzymes & Reducing Agents Flour contains certain enzymes, called proteases or proteinases, which attack gluten protein molecules and break them apart. Another type of gluten relaxer involves reducing agents, such as glutathione, sorbic acid, sodium bisulfite, and L-cysteine. (Cysteine is an amino acid, and is a main ingredient in several brands of pizza dough conditioners.) As with protease enzymes, reducing agents attack gluten molecules and break them apart. However, too much produces an overly slack dough and flat crust. (Flatness is caused by the inability of the gluten to retain crust structure during oven spring and cooling.) The gluten-softening effect of…

However, too much produces an overly slack dough and flat crust. (Flatness is caused by the inability of the gluten to retain crust structure during oven spring and cooling.) The gluten-softening effect of protease accelerates with time.
In doing that they weaken gluten’s elasticity, or cause it to be more “relaxed.” With relaxed gluten, dough is less springy and easier to roll, stretch, and fit to a screen.

However, whereas the effect of protease enzymes is irreversible — that is, the dough becomes more and more relaxed over time — the relaxing effect of a reducing agent, such as cysteine, can be reversed after mixing if an oxidiz-ing agent (see next section)…

entries.title: Oil & Shortening

entries.title: Parbaked Crust
entries.summary: A key to quality parbaked crust is to experiment until you find the best formula, procedures, bake time and temperature, then consistently apply them to every production run. Then, using a conveyor oven, set the bake time for 3-1/2 minutes and adjust the temperature so that the crust comes out as done as possible without starting to brown. You can make a thin press-baked style of parbaked crust by using one of the various models of hot presses.

entries.title: Proofing
entries.summary: With proper yeast portion, dough ball proofing can be accomplished at room temperature (i.e., 80 degrees F). A more elegant solution is a proof box, or proofer, which has temperature and moisture controls. There are two kinds of proof-ing: (1) dough ball proofing and (2) pan or screen proofing.
takeaways: With proper yeast portion, dough ball proofing can be accomplished at room temperature (i.e., 80 degrees F).
A more elegant solution is a proof box, or proofer, which has temperature and moisture controls.
There are two kinds of proof-ing: (1) dough ball proofing and (2) pan or screen proofing.
With dough ball proofing the ball is allowed to rise until it doubles in volume (which is about a 50 percent increase in diameter), then it’s rolled into a pizza and baked immediately.
Proofing is the final stage of dough fermentation and occurs when dough is allowed to rise prior to 4 – Dough-making 300 making it into pizza.

entries.title: Proper Dough Rise
entries.summary: Over-risen dough, which often has a beer-like odor, makes a flat crust with an irregular grain, a gummy-grayish crumb, a white (non-browned) surface that tends to blister, and bland flavor. Properly risen dough produces a crust of maximum volume, even grain, white crumb, golden-brown surface, and full-bodied yeast bread flavor. Dough rising results from fermentation — a process whereby yeast digests sugar and produces CO2 (carbon dioxide) gas in the dough.
takeaways: Over-risen dough, which often has a beer-like odor, makes a flat crust with an irregular grain, a gummy-grayish crumb, a white (non-browned) surface that tends to blister, and bland flavor.
Properly risen dough produces a crust of maximum volume, even grain, white crumb, golden-brown surface, and full-bodied yeast bread flavor.
Dough rising results from fermentation — a process whereby yeast digests sugar and produces CO2 (carbon dioxide) gas in the dough.
However, one factor — namely, temperature — affects fermentation after mixing.
The rule is: For every 18 degrees F (or 10 degrees C) increase in dough temperature (up to 100 degrees F), yeast activity doubles, or increases by 100 percent.

entries.title: Rehydration
entries.summary: Except for instant dry yeast, it’s usually best to rehydrate yeast, or combine it with water before mixing. Because salt inhibits yeast activity, do not add salt to the water until just before mixing. Before adding yeast water to the mixer, give it a stir to disperse the yeast.
takeaways: Except for instant dry yeast, it’s usually best to rehydrate yeast, or combine it with water before mixing.
Because salt inhibits yeast activity, do not add salt to the water until just before mixing.
Before adding yeast water to the mixer, give it a stir to disperse the yeast.
The resulting liquid is called “yeast water” or rehydration water.
Even though instant yeast is typically added dry to the dough mix, it can be rehydrated, as well, if the operator chooses.

entries.title: Storage
entries.summary: Dry mixing, Rehydration, and Usage Unlike other forms of yeast, the typical procedure for adding instant yeast is the dry mix method. INSTANT Dry Yeast Instant dry yeast, referred to as instant yeast, for short, is abbreviated IDY and IY and is also some-times called instant blending, high activity, and quick rising yeast. For dough that’s 75 degrees F or higher, add the yeast to the flour, blend at low speed for 30 seconds, then add the remaining dry ingredients, oil and water, and mix as usual.
takeaways: Dry mixing, Rehydration, and Usage Unlike other forms of yeast, the typical procedure for adding instant yeast is the dry mix method.
INSTANT Dry Yeast Instant dry yeast, referred to as instant yeast, for short, is abbreviated IDY and IY and is also some-times called instant blending, high activity, and quick rising yeast.
For dough that’s 75 degrees F or higher, add the yeast to the flour, blend at low speed for 30 seconds, then add the remaining dry ingredients, oil and water, and mix as usual.
For dough that’s under 75 degrees F, rehydrate the yeast prior to mixing and blend all ingredients at low speed until thoroughly combined, then continue mixing at a higher speed if it’s called for.
They say that rehydrating at a wrong tem-perature leaches materials from the yeast cell, which reduces fermentation and also causes stickier, more extensible dough.

entries.summary: If the rest of the pizza is baked, lift it off the floor on a peel and raise it toward the top heat to finish the top. If you don’t have enough top heat, torch the top briefly with a heat gun/torch.
entries.search_text: Top is underdone If the rest of the pizza is baked, lift it off the floor on a peel and raise it toward the top heat to finish the top. If you don’t have enough top heat, torch the top briefly with a heat gun/torch.  bake modernist

entries.title: Burnt on the bottom
entries.summary: The pizza is getting too much bottom heat. Lower the floor/deck temperature if possible. Alternatively, lift the pizza briefly on the peel or move it to a cooler zone (near the mouth of the oven).
entries.search_text: Burnt on the bottom The pizza is getting too much bottom heat. Lower the floor/deck temperature if possible. Alternatively, lift the pizza briefly on the peel or move it to a cooler zone (near the mouth of the oven).  bake burnt modernist

entries.title: Burnt on one side
entries.summary: Rotate the pizza during baking to even out exposure to hot spots and uneven heat.
entries.search_text: Burnt on one side Rotate the pizza during baking to even out exposure to hot spots and uneven heat.  bake burnt modernist

entries.title: Burnt toppings / ingredients
entries.summary: Reorder assembly so delicate ingredients are protected by sauce or cheese, or add delicate toppings after baking. If the crust is also burning, lower oven temperature.
entries.search_text: Burnt toppings / ingredients Reorder assembly so delicate ingredients are protected by sauce or cheese, or add delicate toppings after baking. If the crust is also burning, lower oven temperature.  bake burnt cheese modernist

entries.title: Caked or burnt flour on the bottom
entries.summary: Too much flour on the dough before loading. If using semolina on the peel, sweep the oven floor because semolina tends to burn.
entries.search_text: Caked or burnt flour on the bottom Too much flour on the dough before loading. If using semolina on the peel, sweep the oven floor because semolina tends to burn.  bake burnt modernist

entries.title: Gel layer / gum line under sauce
entries.summary: A gel layer (gum line) happens when sauce and cheese keep the dough underneath from baking properly: sauce (and, to a lesser extent, cheese) acts as a moisture barrier, leaving the covered dough gummy. Reduce it by putting cheese down first and variegating with sauce, or (for bread-like pizzas) fully prebake the dough, flip in the pan, then sauce/cheese and bake again to eliminate the gel layer.

Reduce it by putting cheese down first and variegating with sauce, or (for bread-like pizzas) fully prebake the dough, flip in the pan, then sauce/cheese and bake again to eliminate the gel layer.  bake cheese gummy modernist

entries.title: Pale crust / browning threshold (Maillard)
entries.summary: Browning accelerates once the crust surface exceeds about 130°C / 265°F. Above that threshold, Maillard reactions produce brown pigments, complex flavor compounds, and a stiff/brittle surface (the main exception is soft-crust Neapolitan). If you’re pale, either the surface never reached that range or it didn’t stay there long enough.
